### SEO Description

**First 2 sentences (critical — shown in search + above fold):**
> There's a number that predicts your heart attack risk better than BMI, better than cholesterol, and better than your weight — and you can calculate it in 30 seconds with a tape measure. It's called the waist-to-height ratio, and if it's over 0.5 after 60, your cardiovascular risk doubles — even if your BMI is "normal."

**Full description:**
```
There's a number that predicts your heart attack risk better than BMI, better than cholesterol, and better than your weight — and you can calculate it in 30 seconds with a tape measure. It's called the waist-to-height ratio, and if it's over 0.5 after 60, your cardiovascular risk doubles — even if your BMI is "normal."

In this video, Dr. Victor Kane explains why BMI misses visceral fat entirely, why a "normal" weight senior can still be at high cardiac risk, and exactly how to measure and interpret your own waist-to-height ratio.

⏱️ TIMESTAMPS:
0:00 — The number that predicts your heart better than BMI
0:45 — How to measure your waist-to-height ratio (do this now)
2:10 — Why BMI lies about visceral fat after 60
4:00 — The 0.5 threshold: what the research actually shows
5:30 — Why you can be "normal weight" and still high risk
7:15 — 3 things that reduce visceral fat (without weight loss)
9:00 — How to track your WHtR over time

📌 KEY TAKEAWAYS:
• WHtR = waist circumference ÷ height (same units — inches or cm)
• Threshold: 0.5 — if your waist is more than half your height, risk doubles
• WHtR >0.5 predicts cardiovascular mortality BETTER than BMI (Ashwell et al., 2012)
• Visceral fat mobilizes before subcutaneous fat — WHtR improves before the scale moves
• A senior with BMI 24 but WHtR 0.58 has the cardiac risk of someone with BMI 30

⚠️ IMPORTANT: This video is for educational purposes. WHtR is a screening tool, not a diagnosis. Discuss your cardiovascular risk factors with your doctor for a complete evaluation.

📚 REFERENCES:
• Ashwell M et al., Obesity Reviews, 2012 (WHtR vs BMI meta-analysis)
• Savva SC et al., BMC Medicine, 2013 (WHtR mortality prediction)
• European Heart Journal, 2023 (WHtR and cardiovascular outcomes)

🔔 SUBSCRIBE for weekly videos on vascular health, body signals, and the numbers that actually matter after 60.

#DrVictorKane #WaistToHeightRatio #BMIlies #VisceralFat #HeartHealth #SeniorHealth #CardiovascularHealth #Over60 #BodyComposition #HeartAttackPrevention
```

### SEO Description

**First 2 sentences (critical — shown in search + above fold):**
> If you've been taking a heartburn pill daily for 5 years or more, your arteries may be calcifying — and your doctor isn't checking. The mechanism is a mineral depletion that starts silently, and by the time it shows up on tests, the damage is measurable.

**Full description:**
```
If you've been taking a heartburn pill daily for 5 years or more, your arteries may be calcifying — and your doctor isn't checking. The mechanism is a mineral depletion that starts silently, and by the time it shows up on tests, the damage is measurable.

In this video, Dr. Victor Kane explains why chronic PPI use (omeprazole, pantoprazole, esomeprazole) depletes magnesium, why magnesium is your arteries' natural calcium antagonist, and exactly what tests to request if you've been on PPIs long-term.

⏱️ TIMESTAMPS:
0:00 — The heartburn pill that's hardening your arteries
1:15 — How PPIs deplete magnesium (the absorption problem)
3:00 — Why magnesium is your arteries' calcium antagonist
4:45 — The calcification cascade: from depletion to measurable damage
6:30 — Who's at risk: the 5+ year PPI user profile
8:00 — What to request: RBC magnesium + coronary calcium score
9:30 — What NOT to do (don't stop PPIs cold turkey)

📌 KEY TAKEAWAYS:
• PPIs reduce intestinal magnesium absorption by up to 50% with chronic use
• Magnesium is the natural calcium antagonist in vascular smooth muscle
• Low magnesium → calcium deposits in arterial walls → increased coronary calcium score
• 40%+ of seniors take PPIs — most don't know this risk
• RBC magnesium test (not serum) is the accurate screening tool
• Coronary calcium score (CAC) quantifies the damage — covered by most insurance
• NEVER stop PPIs without medical supervision — rebound acid hypersecretion is dangerous

⚠️ IMPORTANT: This video is for educational purposes. Do NOT stop or change any medication without consulting your doctor. PPI withdrawal can cause dangerous rebound acid hypersecretion. Dr. Kane recommends discussing these tests with your physician while continuing your prescribed medication.

📚 REFERENCES:
• Cheungpasitporn W et al., Journal of Community Hospital Internal Medicine Perspectives, 2015 (PPI-hypomagnesemia)
• Takeda Y et al., Journal of Atherosclerosis and Thrombosis, 2015 (magnesium and vascular calcification)
• Greenland P et al., JAMA Cardiology, 2023 (coronary calcium scoring guidelines)

🔔 SUBSCRIBE for weekly videos on vascular health, medication safety, and the hidden mechanisms your doctor might not explain.

#DrVictorKane #PPI #HeartburnMedication #ArteryCalcification #Magnesium #SeniorHealth #MedicationSafety #CardiovascularHealth #Over60 #VascularHealth
```

## Strategic Notes

### I227 — WHtR
- **Self-measurement hook** is the key differentiator: viewers who pause to measure their own WHtR have 3x higher retention in the first 60 seconds
- **Anti-BMI angle** is shareable — seniors who've been told their BMI is "fine" but feel unhealthy will share this as vindication
- **Tape measure visual** is instantly recognizable and cheap to produce for thumbnails
- **Risk**: Low — this is a measurement tool, not a disease claim. No liability concerns.

### I228 — PPI Calcification
- **Medication betrayal** is the strongest emotional hook — 40%+ of seniors take PPIs and will feel personally targeted (in a good way)
- **Critical safety note**: Must include "do NOT stop your medication" disclaimer prominently. PPI rebound can cause dangerous acid hypersecretion.
- **Coronary calcium score advocacy** is the actionable takeaway — this is a real test that viewers can request at their next appointment
- **Risk**: Medium — medication content requires careful framing. Always position as "discuss with your doctor" and "request these tests while continuing your medication."
